beats.text: A value function asks one question: starting here and acting forever after, how much reward do I expect to collect?

visual.sub: Recursion turns an infinite sum into a fixed point

beats.text: Recursion makes that infinite sum tractable: a state's value is its immediate reward plus the discounted value of where you land.

visual.tex: V^\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\,[\, r + \gamma\, V^\pi(s') \,]
visual.note: Bellman expectation equation for a fixed policy

beats.text: For control we want the best policy, so the average over the policy becomes a maximum over actions.

visual.tex: V^{*}(s) = \max_a \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\,[\, r + \gamma\, V^{*}(s') \,] = \max_a Q^{*}(s, a)

beats.text: The Bellman operator T takes a guess at the value function and returns a better one; its fixed point is the optimal value.

visual.tex: (\mathcal{T}V)(s) = \max_a \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\,[\, r + \gamma\, V(s') \,]
visual.note: T V* = V* is the fixed point

beats.text: In the backup explorer a near-zero discount makes the higher-reward action win, while a discount near one favors the richer successor.
For a discount below one the operator is a gamma-contraction, which by the Banach theorem guarantees a single value you can iterate to.

visual.label: discount γ: contraction factor per backup

beats.text: Before improving a policy you must score it, computing the value of a fixed policy from the Bellman expectation equation.

visual.sub: Score a fixed policy by iterating one backup

beats.text: Turn the equation into an update rule: plug your current estimate into the right-hand side and read off a new one.

visual.tex: V_{k+1}(s) \leftarrow \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\,[\, r + \gamma\, V_k(s') \,]
visual.note: Iterative policy evaluation, swept until change < θ

beats.text: Because this operator is also a gamma-contraction, the estimates converge to the true value geometrically from any start.
On the four-by-four gridworld under a random policy, corner-adjacent cells settle near minus fourteen and the deepest cells reach about minus twenty-two.
Those numbers are the expected number of steps a random walker takes to stumble out of the grid, negated.

visual.label: deepest interior value under random policy

beats.text: Policy evaluation scores a policy but cannot improve one; the leverage comes from pairing it with a greedy step.
Why fully evaluate a policy you are about to throw away? Value iteration does one backup using the max over actions.

visual.sub: One greedy backup per sweep, no inner loop

beats.text: That is the Bellman optimality operator applied as an update, driving the value straight to optimal without naming any intermediate policy.

visual.tex: V_{k+1}(s) \leftarrow \max_a \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\,[\, r + \gamma\, V_k(s') \,]

beats.text: On the gridworld with no discount this converges in just four sweeps, flooding value outward one ring per sweep.
Convergence rests on contraction: each backup shrinks the worst-case error between any two value tables by at least gamma.

visual.note: T is a γ-contraction in the max-norm

beats.text: After k sweeps the error is bounded by gamma to the k, so the discount alone sets the rate of geometric convergence.
At a discount of zero point nine you lose about one digit of error every twenty-two sweeps; near one the crawl is much slower.

visual.label: sweeps to lose one digit of error at γ=0.9

beats.text: Policy iteration takes the opposite tack: evaluate the current policy completely, then make it greedy with respect to those values.
The improvement step acts greedily one step then follows the old policy, which can only raise the value.

visual.tex: \pi'(s) = \arg\max_a \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\,[\, r + \gamma\, V^\pi(s') \,]

beats.text: The policy improvement theorem guarantees the new policy is at least as good everywhere, with strict gains unless already optimal.
Since a finite MDP has finitely many deterministic policies, the loop terminates exactly; on the gridworld it takes four rounds.

visual.label: improvement rounds to optimal on 4x4 grid

beats.text: Policy and value iteration are endpoints of one spectrum, with modified policy iteration running m evaluation sweeps in between.
Both are generalized policy iteration: evaluation and improvement chase each other until they agree at the optimal pair.
Every backup in this chapter sums over the dynamics, an expectation you can only compute if you know the transition model.

visual.sub: Every backup is an expectation over p(s',r | s,a)

beats.text: You rarely have that model: a robot ships without a transition table and a game agent never sees the opponent's policy.

visual.note: The fingerprint requiring a known model

beats.text: Even with the model each sweep is expensive, costing order states squared times actions for a dense transition model.

visual.note: Per-sweep cost; the curse of dimensionality

beats.text: A robot arm with ten joints discretized to a hundred positions each has a hundred to the tenth power states.

visual.label: states for a 10-joint arm at 100 positions each

beats.text: The escape route is sampling: replace the exact expectation with a transition you actually take and observe.

visual.note: TD learning: EQ R2.4 with one sample and rate α

beats.text: That single swap turns value iteration into Q-learning and keeps the GPI skeleton; DP is the ground truth the rest approximates.
